compiler_connection.notes: T01001 Samuel is the compiler's gen 4 Talcott ancestor. His son T01389 Samuel married Abigail Pantry Hooker — the key Hooker-Talcott intersection.
compiler_connection.path: T01001 → T01389 Samuel × Abigail Pantry Hooker → H01789 Seth → H03203 John Butler → Florence → Helen → Lea → compiler

notable.notable_blurb: Great-grandfather of Adm. George Dewey of Manila Bay

cross_connections.display_label: Both buried in rubble of the 1766 schoolhouse explosion

military_service.notes: FindAGrave Old Eastbury memorial designates him 'Col Samuel Talcott Veteran.' Active in the Revolutionary War period; died Glastonbury CT 16 March 1780, mid-war, leaving his fifteen-year-old son Samuel (T01389) without a father.

burial.plot_notes: Old Eastbury Cemetery, Glastonbury CT. FindAGrave 61929162. Stone surviving intact.

narrative_blocks.header: Revolutionary War colonel from Glastonbury
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel served as a Connecticut militia Colonel in the Revolutionary War — the rank by which his Old Eastbury Cemetery memorial designates him 'Col Samuel Talcott Veteran.' He was the third generation of Talcott military service, his father Capt. Samuel having served in the Connecticut militia of the colonial era and his ancestor Capt. Samuel Talcott having commanded Connecticut dragoons at Deerfield in 1690.

narrative_blocks.header: Four children by Mary, none by Sarah
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel married Mary Smith of Hartford in 1757; she bore him Mary, Hannah, Samuel, and Capt. Nehemiah before her 1770 death at thirty-seven. He remarried Sarah (surname not preserved); she had no children with him and outlived him.

narrative_blocks.header: Died mid-Revolution leaving two young sons
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel died in Glastonbury on 16 March 1780 at age 47, squarely in the middle of the Revolutionary War he had served. Sons Samuel and Nehemiah were just fifteen and thirteen at his death; older daughters Mary and Hannah were already grown. His Glastonbury gravestone survives intact at Old Eastbury Cemetery: 'In Memory of Mr. Samuel Talcott who departed this life March 16th, 1780 Aged 47 years.'

narrative_blocks.header: Descended from the Deerfield dragoon commander
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel's ancestor Capt. Samuel Talcott (b.1635) commanded the Connecticut dragoons at Deerfield Massachusetts at the outbreak of King William's War in 1690. That ancestor's father had served as deputy governor of Connecticut Colony in 1637 and as the colony's treasurer through the 1650s. Three generations of Talcott civic and military service stand between Col. Samuel and the colony's founding.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandfather of the Manila Bay admiral
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel's great-grandson Admiral George Dewey commanded the U.S. fleet at the Battle of Manila Bay in May 1898, sinking all eight Spanish ships with no American deaths and only eight wounded. The Talcott military line continued forward from Samuel's Revolutionary service through three generations to one of the most decisive naval victories in American history.

narrative_blocks.header: Survived the 1766 Hartford schoolhouse explosion
narrative_blocks.body: On 23 May 1766 he was among twenty-eight men buried in the rubble when the Hartford schoolhouse exploded — gunpowder ignited during the town's Stamp Act repeal celebration. He survived with severe burns to his face and arms; six others, including Levi Jones, did not.

compiler_connection.notes: T01001 Samuel is the compiler's gen 4 Talcott ancestor. His son T01389 Samuel married Abigail Pantry Hooker — the key Hooker-Talcott intersection.
compiler_connection.path: T01001 → T01389 Samuel × Abigail Pantry Hooker → H01789 Seth → H03203 John Butler → Florence → Helen → Lea → compiler

burial.plot_notes: Old Eastbury Cemetery, Glastonbury CT. FindAGrave 61929162. Stone surviving intact.

He was the third generation of Talcott military service, his father Capt. Samuel having served in the Connecticut militia of the colonial era and his ancestor Capt. Samuel Talcott having commanded Connecticut dragoons at Deerfield in 1690.

Sons Samuel and Nehemiah were just fifteen and thirteen at his death; older daughters Mary and Hannah were already grown. His Glastonbury gravestone survives intact at Old Eastbury Cemetery: 'In Memory of Mr. Samuel Talcott who departed this life March 16th, 1780 Aged 47 years.'

That ancestor's father had served as deputy governor of Connecticut Colony in 1637 and as the colony's treasurer through the 1650s. Three generations of Talcott civic and military service stand between Col. Samuel and the colony's founding.

The Talcott military line continued forward from Samuel's Revolutionary service through three generations to one of the most decisive naval victories in American history.

narrative_blocks.body: On 23 May 1766 he was among twenty-eight men buried in the rubble when the Hartford schoolhouse exploded — gunpowder ignited during the town's Stamp Act repeal celebration. He survived with severe burns to his face and arms; six others, including Levi Jones, did not.

birth.note: Per family source: 'Mary was born in 1733 in Hartford, CT'

cross_connections.display_label: Settled Vermont 1789 after cousin David Newton's 1777 migration

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Lived on the Perrin family farmstead from her arrival in Berlin in 1789 until her death in 1828.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT — alongside Zachariah.

narrative_blocks.header: Walked to Vermont with two oxen teams
narrative_blocks.body: In February or March 1789 Mary and Zachariah moved their family from Hebron Connecticut to Berlin Vermont — traveling up the Connecticut River with two teams of oxen, then up the White River, switching from wheels to runners at Brookfield, then over a blazed trail. They arrived to find their log cabin's roof had collapsed under three to four feet of snow.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost firstborn daughter Mary in 1798
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's eldest child — also named Mary, born in Hebron — died in 1798. Four months later another daughter was born and named Mary in her sister's memory. The second Mary later married Julius Yemans Dewey and was the mother of Admiral George Dewey, hero of Manila Bay 1898.

sources.label: Vermont Genealogy — Biography of Rev. James Hobart

residence.note: Settled May 1789 from Hebron CT; farmed 1300 acres at Scott Hill/East Road intersection in East Road School District.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Established the 1300-acre East Road farmstead and built permanent buildings here after settling Berlin VT in 1789.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT.

narrative_blocks.header: Settled Berlin Vermont with collapsed-roof cabin
narrative_blocks.body: Zachariah served in the Revolutionary War with the 8th company of the 12th Connecticut militia, then in February-March 1789 led his family from Hebron Connecticut to Berlin Vermont — up the Connecticut River with two teams of oxen, switching from wheels to runners in Brookfield. They found the log cabin he had built the previous summer crushed under three or four feet of snow.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded a 1300-acre farm still in family
narrative_blocks.body: Zachariah's 1300 acres in the East Road School District of Berlin VT — at what became the southeast corner of East Road and Scott Hill Road — passed down through generations of Perrins. His son Porter, the first boy born in Berlin, built the house at Scott Hill and Airport Road in 1815 (still standing). A descendant Henry Perrin still lived in 'Perrin territory' along Scott Hill Road into the modern era.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT — FindAGrave 90950772.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Built the family farmhouse in 1815 at Scott Hill Road and Airport Road.
Born and raised on the Perrin family farmstead at East Road and Scott Hill Road.

narrative_blocks.header: First male child born in Berlin Vermont
narrative_blocks.body: Porter was born February 1, 1790, less than a year after his parents Zachariah Perrin and Mary Talcott Perrin migrated by ox-sled from Hebron, Connecticut, to settle the eastern frontier of Berlin. He married Lucy Kinney of Plainfield in November 1815, lived his entire life in Berlin, and died there in May 1871 at age eighty-one.

narrative_blocks.header: Distributed his fortune to charity and his children
narrative_blocks.body: Porter accumulated more property in farming than any other man before him in Berlin, according to the 1881 Vermont Historical Gazetteer. He gave a large share to charitable and religious purposes during his lifetime, distributed much of the rest among his many children, and left enough for his widow's support to her death.

death.cause: died at age thirty-one. Berlin VT history (Child 1888) attributes death to alcoholism that began as a medical prescription: his attending physician prescribed brandy as treatment for the ill health he developed in South Carolina, and the resulting addiction killed him. Talcott Pedigree (1876) silent on cause. Source-discrepancy preserved as historical record.

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave memorial 103993.

education.notes: Class of 1812 per Talcott Pedigree (1876); Berlin VT history (Child 1888) gives 1813. Discrepancy unresolved.

career.notes: Preached in various places in Vermont, New York, and Pennsylvania.
Three-year pastorate in South Carolina taken for health reasons; did not recover; returned to Berlin.

narrative_blocks.header: Pastor in South Carolina sent home to die
narrative_blocks.body: Poor health led Rev. Perrin to accept a three-year pastorate in South Carolina, hoping a warmer climate would restore him. He did not recover and returned to Berlin Vermont, where he died in February 1824 at age thirty-one.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote two volumes of poetry before age thirty-one
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Perrin published two volumes of poetry as a Congregational minister preaching in Vermont, New York, and Pennsylvania. A third volume titled Eloida is rumored but no extant copy has been found.

narrative_blocks.header: Brandy prescribed as medicine became fatal addiction
narrative_blocks.body: Per the 1888 Berlin Vermont history, Rev. Perrin's attending physician prescribed brandy as a medical treatment for the ill health that drove him from his South Carolina pastorate back to Berlin. The prescription created an addiction that the source frankly attributes as the cause of his death at thirty-one.

burial.plot_notes: East Road Cemetery, Berlin VT — solo Hooker-tree connection, no CEM entry built per §0.4 threshold. Note: Cemetery sits adjacent to historic Perrin family land at East Road / Scott Hill intersection in Berlin VT.

narrative_blocks.header: Berlin Vermont son who took the gospel south
narrative_blocks.body: Truman was born in Berlin Vermont in 1796 to the Perrin family's newly-cleared 1300-acre Scott Hill farm. He took the Presbyterian ministry as his calling and migrated south through Alabama and Mississippi where his three children grew up — but he died in Massachusetts in 1869, and was returned to Vermont for burial near the family land where he had been born.

narrative_blocks.header: Southern bank failures ruined him by 1850
narrative_blocks.body: After years preaching and accumulating merchant property in Alabama and Georgia, the failure of several Southern banks around 1850 wiped out most of his fortune. He returned to Vermont and spent his remaining nineteen years 'preaching in various places, and in the employ of the American Tract Society.'

narrative_blocks.header: Wife stayed in Aberdeen Mississippi when he returned
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Pronecy Tindall remained in Aberdeen Mississippi when he moved back north around 1850. He died at Washington Massachusetts in November 1869; she lived another fifteen years in Aberdeen, dying there in 1884.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried Vermont family land though died Massachusetts
narrative_blocks.body: Despite dying at Washington Berkshire County Massachusetts in November 1869, he was buried at East Road Cemetery in Berlin Vermont — adjacent to the 1300-acre Perrin family land his father Zachariah had established in 1789. His parents had chosen a different Berlin cemetery; Truman's plot lay closer to the farm.

career.notes: Per Pronecy's 1884 obituary 'the late Presbyterian minister.' Migration history: Berlin VT (b.1796) → Alabama (children born early 1830s) → Mississippi (raised William in Aberdeen) → Massachusetts (d.1869 Washington Berkshire County).

burial.plot_notes: Green Mount Cemetery, Montpelier VT — alongside Julius.

narrative_blocks.header: Burned the log cabin where she was born
narrative_blocks.body: Per family lore, Mary Polly was 'ashamed of having been born in a log cabin' — she and a sister later went up one night and burned the original Perrin cabin to the ground. Her father Zachariah's permanent farm at Scott Hill in Berlin Vermont stood on the same land, but the dirt-floor structure of her birth was gone by her own hand.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Manila Bay's Admiral, died at forty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Polly bore Admiral George Dewey on December 26, 1837 — her third child. He was five years old when she died at age 44 in September 1843 Montpelier Vermont. Her husband Julius later married twice more, but Mary Polly was the mother of all his children.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her elder sister Mary Perrin (1781-1798), born Hebron CT, who died at age seventeen — four months before her parents named the second baby Mary in her memory. The second Mary was sometimes called Polly to distinguish from her dead namesake.

burial.plot_notes: Hockanum Cemetery, East Hartford, Hartford County, Connecticut. Buried near her husband Epaphras Hills Sr. (X01833) and other Hills family members.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived eighty-nine years across nine American presidencies
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah was born in 1759 in colonial Glastonbury Connecticut and married Epaphras Hills at twenty-one in September 1780, while the Revolution was still being fought. She bore seven children spanning twenty-four years and died in East Hartford in 1848, during the year that ended the Mexican-American War.

burial.plot_notes: Hockanum Cemetery, East Hartford, Hartford County, Connecticut.
Center Cemetery, South Windsor, Hartford County, Connecticut. FindAGrave memorial 15520935.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived ninety years in South Windsor Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Epaphras Jr. was born in East Hartford Connecticut and died ninety years later in South Windsor in May 1869. Source records his birth as April 1779, predating his parents' documented September 1780 marriage by about seventeen months — a discrepancy that may reflect a misrecord or premarital birth.

burial.plot_notes: Hockanum Cemetery, East Hartford, Hartford County, Connecticut. FindAGrave memorial 74503994.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived ninety-three years in East Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah was named for her mother Hannah Talcott Hills and lived ninety-three years in East Hartford Connecticut, born just after the Revolution and dying in 1878 after the Civil War. Her marital status is not recorded in available sources.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of a Washington Territory governor
narrative_blocks.body: Laura's son George Edward Cole became the sixth Governor of Washington Territory in 1867 and the territory's fifth Delegate to Congress. She lived to see his 1862 election to Congress; she died in 1865, two years before his governor's appointment.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of long-lived Sophia Brewer Risley
narrative_blocks.body: Fanny was born in East Hartford in 1792 and married David I. Brewer. Her daughter Sophia was born in 1816 and lived ninety years, founding the Risley-Wratten-Stapleton line in Madison County New York.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-eight with no marriage recorded
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice was the fifth Hills sibling, born in 1795 in East Hartford. She died there in 1833 at thirty-eight. No marriage or children are recorded in available sources, though her mother had named her for the family pattern of saints' names that ran through the Talcott line.

burial.plot_notes: Wethersfield Village Cemetery, Wethersfield, Hartford County, Connecticut. FindAGrave memorial 19541425.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his fourth cousin Amelia Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: David and his wife Amelia were fourth cousins through their common ancestor Captain Samuel Talcott of Wethersfield. Both descended five generations down parallel branches of the Talcott line and met as adults from the same Hartford-area network.

burial.plot_notes: Hockanum Cemetery, East Hartford, Hartford County, Connecticut. FindAGrave memorial 48126505.

naming_inspiration.description: Given name honors the maternal Talcott line, particularly his grandfather Col. Samuel Talcott of Glastonbury Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Given his maternal surname as first name
narrative_blocks.body: Talcott Hills was given his maternal grandfather Colonel Samuel Talcott's surname as his first name — a deliberate naming choice that carried the Talcott line forward into a Hills generation. He outlived three of his four documented children.

compiler_connection.notes: Samuel Talcott's marriage to Abigail Pantry Hooker is the key Talcott-Hooker intersection in the compiler's maternal line. Their son Seth (H01789) → John Butler (H03203) → Florence → Helen → Lea Hooker → compiler.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: T01389 Samuel × HD0064 Abigail Pantry Hooker → H01789 Seth → H03203 John B. → Florence → Helen → Lea → compiler

primary_residence.notes: Resided in Eastbury (Glastonbury) until 1788, then West Hartford for the rest of his life.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms. Buried with wife H00687 Abigail Pantry Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Married the Hooker-Talcott convergence in 1795
narrative_blocks.body: On January 25, 1795 he married Abigail Pantry Hooker in West Hartford. He was a fifth-generation Talcott; she was a fifth-generation descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved from Glastonbury to West Hartford in 1788
narrative_blocks.body: He left the Talcott homeland in Eastbury for West Hartford seven years before his marriage. He stayed there for the rest of his life.

classification.lineage_note: Thomas¹ → Samuel²(H00007) → John³ → Roger⁴ → Thomas⁵ → Abigail⁶ Pantry Hooker
classification.intersection_note: Descends from Thomas Hooker (paternal) AND marries into Talcott line (T01389). Compiler descends from her through Talcott line.

compiler_connection.notes: Abigail Pantry Hooker is the compiler's generation 6 ancestor on the maternal Talcott line. Her marriage to Samuel Talcott (T01389) on Jan 25, 1795 is the Hooker-Talcott intersection that gives the compiler his middle name 'Talcott'. Her son Seth Talcott (H01789) → John Butler Talcott (H03203) → Florence H. Talcott → Helen Talcott Hope → Lea Hooker → compiler.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: HD0064 Abigail Pantry Hooker × T01389 Samuel Talcott → H01789 Seth → H03203 John Butler → Florence → Helen → Lea → compiler

open_questions.question: EH entry number for Abigail Pantry Hooker
open_questions.notes: Talcott 1876 identifies her as 5th generation from Thomas Hooker. EH entry number unknown — currently HD0064. Once found, rename to H#####.
open_questions.action: Search EH for children of Thomas Hart Hooker (who held Skene prisoners, LM005); find Abigail Pantry Hooker entry

primary_residence.notes: Born West Hartford; lived there with husband Samuel Talcott after 1788; died there 1854.

narrative_blocks.header: Married the Hooker-Talcott convergence in 1795
narrative_blocks.body: On January 25, 1795 she married Samuel Talcott of Glastonbury. She was a fifth-generation Hooker; he was a fifth-generation Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father's freed man left her his estate
narrative_blocks.body: Bristow had been freed by her father Thomas Hart Hooker at the news of Lexington in April 1775. His 1791 will bequeathed his estate to her and her brother.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died at Roxbury in the Continental Army
narrative_blocks.body: He freed his enslaved man Bristow at news of the Battle of Lexington and enlisted with a Farmington volunteer company. He sickened and died near Boston in winter 1775-76, buried at Roxbury.

narrative_blocks.header: Eighty-four years in West Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: She was born and died in West Hartford, where she stayed for the entirety of her marriage and widowhood. She is buried in Old Center Cemetery beside her husband.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms. Buried with husband T01389 Samuel Talcott III.

bio.notes: 'Pantry' appears to be a middle name, possibly a family name. EH entry number unknown — using HD prefix until confirmed.

burial.plot_notes: Bridgehampton Cemetery, Bridgehampton, Suffolk County NY. FindAGrave confirms. Died 22 days after her son Samuel was born — possible death in childbirth or post-partum complications.

narrative_blocks.header: Died 22 days after her son Samuel was
narrative_blocks.body: Eliza Talcott Francis married Rev. Amzi Francis on May 10, 1824 in West Hartford and moved with him to Bridgehampton, Long Island. Her son Samuel Talcott Francis was born July 26, 1829. Twenty-two days later, on August 17, Eliza died at age 33.

narrative_blocks.header: Sampler records the whole family in silk thread
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1815, when she was about nineteen, Eliza stitched a needlework sampler recording the births of her parents and all six siblings — including a note in her own hand: "my dear little brother John died Oct 17 1815 aged 4 years and 9 months." The sampler is held by the Connecticut Historical Society in Hartford.

cross_connections.display_label: Married his cousin Cynthia Olcott of the Roger Hooker line

burial.plot_notes: North Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived three wives in West Hartford Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel married three times and outlived two of them. Cynthia Olcott — his Hooker-line cousin — died at twenty-three the year after their wedding. Lucelia Brace bore him seven children before dying in 1839.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his Hooker first cousin once removed
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel's mother Abigail Pantry Hooker descended from Thomas Hooker through his son Samuel; his first wife Cynthia Olcott descended from Thomas Hooker through Roger Hooker. Their marriage in 1822 united two parallel Hooker lines that had run separately for five generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost five children before they reached adulthood
narrative_blocks.body: Roger Hooker died in infancy in 1827. Sarah died at thirteen in 1851. Oscar Llewellyn died at seven in 1852.

compiler_connection.notes: Seth Talcott is the compiler's direct Talcott ancestor generation 7. His son H03203 John Butler Talcott → Florence H. → Helen Talcott Hope → Lea Hooker → compiler.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: H01789 Seth → H03203 John Butler → Florence H. → Helen Talcott Hope → Lea Hooker → compiler

primary_residence.notes: Born and died West Hartford. Charlotte his widow died Hartford Jan 4, 1875.

burial.plot_notes: North Cemetery, West Hartford, Hartford County, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Named three of seven children for Hooker ancestry
narrative_blocks.body: Seth Talcott Sr. and Charlotte Stout Butler had seven children. Among them: Seth Jr. (father of painter Allen Butler Talcott), and John B. Talcott — who would found the New Britain Museum of American Art through the Talcott Art Fund.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived eighty-six years unmarried in West Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Talcott, daughter of Samuel Talcott III and Abigail Pantry Hooker, never married. She lived in West Hartford her entire life, born 1806 and dying January 18, 1893 at age 86 — outliving all her siblings.

burial.plot_notes: Old Center Cemetery, West Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost an eight-year-old on her birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Henry and Elizabeth's third child Abigail Hooker Talcott was born April 15, 1848 and died on her eighth birthday, April 15, 1856. The Henry Talcott household lost this daughter in West Hartford the same year his elder brother Sam IV's daughter Sarah Whiting Talcott had also died at thirteen, only five years earlier.

burial.plot_notes: Talcott Cemetery, Lanesborough, Berkshire County MA.

narrative_blocks.header: Migrated to Lanesborough at twenty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Capt. Nehemiah Talcott was born in Glastonbury Connecticut in 1766 and migrated to Lanesborough Massachusetts by 1790 — the year of the first US Census, which shows him already living there. He married Tirzah Platt that August in her hometown and they raised ten children in Lanesborough. He died there in 1848 at eighty-two.

narrative_blocks.header: Died three months after his wife
narrative_blocks.body: Tirzah Platt Talcott died in Lanesborough on July 14, 1848 at seventy-seven. Capt. Nehemiah followed her exactly three months later on October 13, 1848 at eighty-two. They were buried together in Talcott Cemetery — the Lanesborough burying ground that bore the family name.

burial.plot_notes: Talcott Cemetery, Lanesborough, Berkshire County MA. FindAGrave 112083228.

narrative_blocks.header: Brought her husband to her hometown
narrative_blocks.body: Tirzah Platt was born in Lanesborough Massachusetts in 1771. When she married Capt. Nehemiah Talcott in August 1790 he had already migrated west from Glastonbury Connecticut and was living in Lanesborough by the time of the Census. She gave him ten children and never left her hometown — dying there in July 1848.

burial.plot_notes: Talcott Cemetery, Lanesborough, Berkshire County MA.
Talcott Cemetery, Lanesborough, Berkshire County MA.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried five Butler children before her death
narrative_blocks.body: Pamela Talcott Butler outlived five of her seven children. Two died in infancy; three more died as young adults from various causes. Only her surviving daughters carried the line forward through their own descendants.

burial.plot_notes: Talcott Cemetery, Lanesborough, Berkshire County MA.
Talcott Cemetery, Lanesborough MA. FindAGrave 112083056.
Riverside Cemetery, Lanesborough, Berkshire County MA.

narrative_blocks.header: Replacement-named for older sister who died
narrative_blocks.body: Tirzah Ophelia Sherman was named for her elder sister Tirzah, who had died in infancy in 1819. The naming was a common nineteenth-century replacement pattern — keeping a lost child's name alive in the next-born of the same sex.

burial.plot_notes: Talcott Cemetery, Lanesborough, Berkshire County MA.

narrative_blocks.header: Continued Capt. Nehemiah's line through Caroline
narrative_blocks.body: William Augustus Talcott was Capt. Nehemiah's youngest son. Through his daughter Caroline's marriage to Manton Wood the Talcott line extended into the Wood-Sherman families and ultimately west to Utah and California.

compiler_connection.notes: T00458 Samuel Talcott is the compiler's gen 3 Talcott ancestor. Chain: T00458 → T01001 Samuel → T01389 Samuel × Abigail Pantry Hooker → H01789 Seth → H03203 John Butler → Florence → Helen → Lea → compiler.

primary_residence.notes: Lived and died in Glastonbury on the family homestead.

burial.plot_notes: Green Cemetery, Glastonbury, Hartford County, Connecticut.

will.summary: Will of Lt. Benjamin Talcott, Glastonbury CT. Wife: £80 + use of parlor, cellar, garret, ovens, gardens, one-third of orchard and land west of highway to great river — during widowhood only. Son Benjamin: lands in Bolton + 124 acres Hebron (bought of Joseph and John Phelps). Son John: 300 acres Hebron (west of Jacob Root), 50 acres Hebron (north of Richard Curtis), one yoke of steers, £10 moveable, + £15 from Elizur. Son Samuel: south half of home lot, 153 acres beyond Black Ledge River (Glastonbury/Hebron border), half undivided Glastonbury lands, smith-shop with all smithery tools, longest gun, half team. Son Elizur: north half of home lot with all buildings (except smith-shop), 60 acres Ash Swamp Glastonbury, 96 acres near bare hill Glastonbury, other half undivided lands, newest gun, half team — he to pay John £15. Daughter Sarah: £100. Daughter Hannah: £100 + decent suit of clothes or £10. Daughter Mehitabel: £100 + decent suit of clothes or £10. Executors: son-in-law Jonathan Hale and son Benjamin Talcott. Witnesses: Samuel Smith Jr., Nathaniel Talcott, Thomas Wells. Estate inventory: £2563 15s. 8d.
will.note: Will text reproduced in Talcott Pedigree (1876) pp.106-109.

cross_connections.display_label: Glastonbury homestead passed intact to him 175 years later.

narrative_blocks.header: His house bore bullet marks until 1854
narrative_blocks.body: He built a fortified wooden house on Main Street, Glastonbury in 1699 — a refuge from Indian attacks for the surrounding community. The house bore bullet marks until it was taken down soon after 1854.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Sarah died in childbed at thirty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hollister, granddaughter of original 1636 Hartford settler John Hollister, married him in 1699 and bore him eight children. She died in childbirth at the homestead October 15, 1715.

narrative_blocks.header: Sons signed Bolton's 1731 founding patent
narrative_blocks.body: Two of his sons Benjamin and Nathaniel were listed as Bolton Proprietors in the 1731 patent, signed by their cousin Governor Joseph Talcott. A third son Elizur later chaired Glastonbury's denunciation of the Boston Port Bill.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Wright cousins married two Talcott siblings
narrative_blocks.body: His son Elizur married Ruth Wright; his daughter Mehitabel married Ruth's first cousin Hezekiah Wright. Two Wright cousins thus married two Talcott siblings, weaving the families together.

narrative_blocks.header: Farm in family from 1643 through 1876
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather John Talcott had bought the Glastonbury farm from Samuel Sherman and Richard Gildersleeve in 1643. The land was still owned by descendant Jared G. Talcott in 1876.

burial.plot_notes: Green Cemetery, Glastonbury, Hartford County, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: She died in childbed; the baby followed
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hollister married Deacon Benjamin Talcott and died in childbed at Glastonbury in 1715, at thirty-eight. The daughter whose birth she did not survive, Abigail, lived only eighteen days more.

burialCemetery.name: Old Eastbury Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Glastonbury CT cemetery. Burial site of Col. Samuel Talcott (T01001, 1732-1780), whose Revolutionary War-era gravestone survives intact. His first wife Mary Smith Talcott (U00042, d.1770) is unconfirmed but per family tradition likely buried here too, having predeceased him by ten years. Resting place of John House IV (X01233, 1758-1798, age 39-40) — husband of Esther Hooker House. He died in Hartford in November 1798 leaving Esther a widow at thirty-nine with seven living children (and Cyprian an infant of about eighteen months). She lived another fifty years and died in 1848 at age eighty-nine. Esther herself is buried at Old Eastbury per FG memorial — though her primary residence shifted to East Hartford after John IV's death. [Merged from duplicate CEM246, Stage 6Y-652.]

crossConnections.display_label: Both buried in rubble of the 1766 schoolhouse explosion
